title: Shape of Data Distributions
why: To describe a distribution's shape — where the data clumps, where the peak is, whether it leans left or right — you must first read a number line and a bar/dot plot, find the tallest bar, and compare counts. Warm those feeders and shape talk makes sense.

diagnostic.q: On a number line, which value is farthest to the right: 2, 7, 4?
Counts at each value are 1, 4, 2. Which value has the most (the peak)?
Which is more, a bar of 6 or a bar of 3?

learn.heading: Reading where data piles up
learn.intro: The shape of data tells you where values cluster on a number line. The peak is the tallest bar (the most common value). Comparing heights left to right shows if the data leans one way.
examples: Peak: if counts are 2, 5, 3, the peak is at the value with 5 — that's the most common one.
Leaning: if the tall bars are on the left and short bars on the right, the data clusters low. Reading the number line left-to-right shows the trend.

items.q: A. Counts are 1, 3, 2. What is the biggest count?
B. Which is more, 5 or 2? Type the bigger number.
C. On a number line, the points are 1, 2, 3. Which is farthest right?

tiers.intro: Find the peak and compare sides.

items.q: A. A dot plot has counts 1, 2, 6, 2, 1 across values 1–5. Where is the peak?
items.hint: The tallest count is 6, which sits at value 3.

items.q: B. Counts on the left side total 2 + 1 = ___

tiers.intro: Compare two sides of a distribution.

items.q: A. Left bars are 3 and 2; right bars are 1 and 1. What is the left total?
B. Left total is 5 and right total is 2. Which side has more data?
items.hint: 5 is greater than 2, so the data clusters on the left.

exit.q: 1. Counts are 2, 7, 4. What is the biggest count (the peak height)?
2. A number line has dots piled up near the low numbers with few high ones. The data clusters where?
exit.hint: Most dots are over the low values, so it clusters low (left).
